qa_pairs.question: What information should I provide to support if I've lost all my registered multifactor authentication devices and don't have bypass codes?
qa_pairs.answer: If you've lost all your registered devices and don't have bypass codes, you should email support@tech.alliancecan.ca with the following information: your primary email address, how long your account has been active, your research area, your IP address, your principal investigator, your group members, a contact for validation, which clusters you use most often, which software modules you load most often, when you ran your last job, a few of your latest batch job IDs, and ticket topics and IDs from your recent technical support requests.

qa_pairs.question: Which SSH clients are compatible with multifactor authentication?
qa_pairs.answer: Most command-line SSH clients, such as those on Linux and macOS, are compatible. Specific clients that also support multifactor authentication include Cyberduck, FileZilla, JuiceSSH on Android, MobaXTerm, PuTTY, PyCharm, Termius on iOS, VSCode, and WinSCP.

qa_pairs.question: What details about my usage on clusters should I include when contacting support about a lost MFA device?
qa_pairs.answer: When contacting support about a lost MFA device, you should include which clusters you use the most, which software modules you load most often on the clusters, when you ran your last job on the clusters, and provide a few of your latest batch job IDs on the clusters.

qa_pairs.question: Who should I name as a contact for validation when reporting a lost MFA device?
qa_pairs.answer: You should name someone whom the support team can contact to validate your request.

qa_pairs.question: Are there specific mobile SSH clients that work with multifactor authentication?
qa_pairs.answer: Yes, JuiceSSH on Android and Termius on iOS are specific mobile SSH clients that work with multifactor authentication.
